reasoning: The CSS injection pattern exists in code: sanitize_text_field() does not prevent } or </style> from appearing in CSS property values interpolated into the <style> block in admin_head() (lines 97-113). The wp_kses_wf() output sanitizer calls wp_kses() which allows the <style> tag (explicitly listed in allowed_tags at line 467) and does not sanitize the inner CSS text content of that block, so malicious CSS values would reach the browser. However, the attack is not practically exploitable: all config values (icon_right, icon_bottom, icon_border, icon_padding, icon_size, menu_accent_color, custom_css) are hardcoded in config.php as PHP literals. They are not stored in the database and are not settable through any WordPress admin UI or AJAX endpoint. An attacker must have direct filesystem write access to config.php to inject a malicious value. Filesystem write access is equivalent to arbitrary code execution — at that privilege level, CSS injection provides no meaningful uplift over simply writing malicious PHP. The co-plugin monkey-patching scenario (modifying $config after require_once) is also a code-execution-level primitive. Because the attacker prerequisite (filesystem write or co-installed malicious plugin with code exec) already subsumes the impact of the claimed CSS injection XSS, the finding does not represent an independently exploitable vulnerability. Marking is_real=false: the code pattern is imperfect but the finding as a security vulnerability is not valid given the required attacker capability.
